html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Four

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 73 through 89

Book III — The Psalms of Asaph and the Sons of Qorah

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 5-71 fragments

4Q86 (4QPsd) — Cave 4 — Psalms 48, 76, 78, 81

4Q88 (4QPsf) — Cave 4 — Psalms 22, 107, 109

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK III — PSALMS 73 THROUGH 89 — COMPLETE

TEHILLIM — PSALM 73 Of Asaph — Truly Creator Is Good — The Prosperity of the Wicked and the Nearness of Creator

1 Truly Creator is good to Yisra'EL, to those who are pure in heart.

2 But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled, my steps had nearly slipped.

3 For I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.

4 For they have no pangs until death — their bodies are fat and sleek.

5 They are not in trouble as others are — they are not stricken like the rest of mankind.

6 Therefore pride is their necklace — violence covers them as a garment.

7 Their eyes swell out through fatness — their hearts overflow with follies.

8 They scoff and speak with malice — loftily they threaten oppression.

9 They set their mouths against the heavens, and their tongue struts through the earth.

10 Therefore his people turn back to them, and find no fault in them.

11 And they say, How can Creator know? Is there knowledge in the Most High?

12 Behold, these are the wicked — always at ease, they increase in riches.

13 All in vain have I kept my heart clean and washed my hands in innocence.

14 For all the day long I have been stricken and rebuked every morning.

15 If I had said, I will speak thus, I would have betrayed the generation of your children.

16 But when I thought how to understand this, it seemed to me a wearisome task,

17 Until I went into the sanctuary of Creator — then I discerned their end.

18 Truly you set them in slippery places — you make them fall to ruin.

19 How they are destroyed in a moment, swept away utterly by terrors.

20 Like a dream when one awakes, O YHWH, when you rouse yourself, you despise them as phantoms.

21 When my soul was embittered, when I was pricked in heart,

22 I was brutish and ignorant — I was like a beast toward you.

23 Nevertheless, I am continually with you — you hold my right hand.

24 You guide me with your counsel, and afterward you will receive me to glory.

25 Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you.

26 My flesh and my heart may fail, but Creator is the strength of my heart and my portion forever.

27 For behold, those who are far from you shall perish — you put an end to everyone who is unfaithful to you.

28 But for me it is good to be near Creator — I have made YHWH my refuge, that I may tell of all your works.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 73 opens Book III of the Tehillim and is the first of the Asaph psalms in this collection. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 73 in full. The psalm is read as the paradigmatic account of the covenant person's crisis of faith — the stumbling point of envy at the wicked — resolved only by entering the sanctuary of Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 73. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — until I went into the sanctuary of Creator — then I discerned their end. The entire crisis of Psalm 73 — the near-stumbling of verses 2-16 — is resolved in one act: entering the sanctuary. What reasoning could not solve, presence with YHWH resolved. The sanctuary gives the psalmist the perspective of eternity — from within YHWH's presence the prosperity of the wicked is seen as transient and the nearness of YHWH as the only true good. Verse 25 — whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you. The psalmist who nearly stumbled because of the wicked's prosperity ends by declaring that YHWH alone satisfies — not wealth, not ease, not the prosperity he once envied.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 74 A Maskil of Asaph — Why Do You Cast Us Off Forever — How Long Will the Enemy Scoff?

1 O Creator, why do you cast us off forever? Why does your anger smoke against the sheep of your pasture?

2 Remember your congregation, which you have purchased of old, which you have redeemed to be the tribe of your heritage — remember Mount Tziyon, where you have dwelt.

3 Direct your steps to the perpetual ruins — the enemy has destroyed everything in the sanctuary.

4 Your foes have roared in the midst of your meeting place — they set up their own signs for signs.

5 They were like those who swing axes in a forest of trees.

6 And all its carved wood they broke down with hatchets and hammers.

7 They set your sanctuary on fire — they profaned the dwelling place of your name, bringing it down to the ground.

8 They said to themselves, We will utterly subdue them — they burned all the meeting places of Creator in the land.

9 We do not see our signs — there is no longer any prophet — and there is none among us who knows how long.

10 How long, O Creator, is the foe to scoff? Is the enemy to revile your name forever?

11 Why do you hold back your hand, your right hand? Take it from the fold of your garment and destroy them.

12 Yet Creator my King is from of old, working salvation in the midst of the earth.

13 You divided the sea by your might — you broke the heads of the sea monsters on the waters.

14 You crushed the heads of Leviatan — you gave him as food for the creatures of the wilderness.

15 You split open springs and brooks — you dried up ever-flowing streams.

16 Yours is the day, yours also the night — you have established the heavenly lights and the sun.

17 You have fixed all the boundaries of the earth — you have made summer and winter.

18 Remember this, O YHWH, how the enemy scoffs, and a foolish people reviles your name.

19 Do not deliver the soul of your dove to the wild beasts — do not forget the life of your poor forever.

20 Have regard for the covenant, for the dark places of the land are full of the habitations of violence.

21 Let not the downtrodden turn back in shame — let the poor and needy praise your name.

22 Arise, O Creator, defend your cause — remember how the foolish scoff at you all the day.

23 Do not forget the clamor of your foes, the uproar of those who rise against you, which goes up continually.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 74 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a communal lament over the destruction of the sanctuary — likely the Babylonian destruction of the First Temple. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 74 in full. The appeal to YHWH's creation power — verses 13-17 — in the context of the sanctuary's destruction is read as the covenant argument: the Creator who established the cosmos can surely restore his own dwelling place.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 74. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — we do not see our signs — there is no longer any prophet — and there is none among us who knows how long. The triple absence: no signs, no prophet, no knowledge of the end. This is the condition of maximum covenant desolation — YHWH appears absent, his messengers are gone, and no timeline is given. The Qumran community identified with this condition — they understood themselves as living in the time between the last prophet and the eschatological restoration. Verse 20 — have regard for the covenant. The ultimate appeal is not to YHWH's power or to the community's righteousness but to his own covenant commitment. The covenant is YHWH's self-obligation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 75 Of Asaph — A Song — A Psalm — We Give Thanks to You — Creator Judges with Equity

1 We give thanks to you, O Creator — we give thanks, for your name is near. We recount your wondrous deeds.

2 At the set time that I appoint I will judge with equity.

3 When the earth totters, and all its inhabitants, it is I who keep steady its pillars.

4 I say to the boastful, Do not boast, and to the wicked, Do not lift up your horn.

5 Do not lift up your horn on high — do not speak with haughty neck.

6 For not from the east or from the west and not from the wilderness comes lifting up,

7 But it is Creator who executes judgment, putting down one and lifting up another.

8 For in the hand of YHWH there is a cup with foaming wine, well mixed, and he pours out from it — and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it down to the dregs.

9 But I will declare it forever — I will sing praises to the Creator of Yaakov.

10 All the horns of the wicked I will cut off, but the horns of the righteous shall be lifted up.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 75 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is structured as a divine oracle — Creator speaks directly in verses 2-5 — followed by human response. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 75 in full. Verse 7 — it is Creator who executes judgment, putting down one and lifting up another — is read as the covenant foundation of political theology: no human power rises or falls except by YHWH's sovereign act.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 75. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — at the set time that I appoint I will judge with equity. YHWH himself speaks — the judgment will come, but at his appointed time, not at the time of human demand. The mo'ed — the set time — is covenant language: YHWH's appointed times govern both the calendar and the schedule of his judgments. The Qumran community's commitment to the 364-day solar calendar was inseparable from their conviction that YHWH's judgments fall at his appointed times. Verse 8 — in the hand of YHWH there is a cup with foaming wine — all the wicked of the earth shall drain it down to the dregs. The cup of divine wrath is a recurring prophetic image — Yirmeyahu 25:15, Yehezkel 23:31.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 76 Of Asaph — A Song — A Psalm — Creator Is Known in Yehudah — He Cuts Off the Spirit of Princes

1 In Yehudah Creator is known — his name is great in Yisra'EL.

2 His abode has been established in Shalem, his dwelling place in Tziyon.

3 There he broke the flashing arrows, the shield, the sword, and the weapons of war.

4 Glorious are you, more majestic than the mountains full of prey.

5 The stouthearted were stripped of their spoil — they sank into sleep — all the men of war were unable to use their hands.

6 At your rebuke, O Creator of Yaakov, both rider and horse lay stunned.

7 But you, you are to be feared — who can stand before you when once your anger is roused?

8 From the heavens you uttered judgment — the earth feared and was still,

9 When Creator arose to establish judgment, to save all the humble of the earth.

10 Surely the wrath of man shall praise you — the remnant of wrath you will put on like a belt.

11 Make your vows to YHWH your Creator and perform them — let all around him bring gifts to him who is to be feared,

12 Who cuts off the spirit of princes, who is to be feared by the kings of the earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 76 is attested in 4QPsd (4Q86) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a Tziyon hymn celebrating YHWH's decisive victory over the enemies of his people — likely reflecting the Assyrian threat against Yerushalaym. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 76 in full. Verse 9 — when Creator arose to establish judgment, to save all the humble of the earth — is read as the paradigmatic act of YHWH's intervention: he arises to judge and to save simultaneously.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 76. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — surely the wrath of man shall praise you — the remnant of wrath you will put on like a belt. Human wrath — even the wrath of YHWH's enemies — ultimately serves his purposes and becomes praise to him. Nothing in creation, including the rage of the wicked, falls outside YHWH's sovereign governance. Verse 12 — who cuts off the spirit of princes, who is to be feared by the kings of the earth. The divine warrior who scattered armies also cuts off the breath of those who hold the highest earthly power. No principality or human authority stands against YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 77 Of Asaph — To Jeduthun — I Cry Aloud to Creator — I Will Remember the Deeds of YHWH

1 I cry aloud to Creator, aloud to Creator, and he will hear me.

2 In the day of my trouble I seek YHWH — in the night my hand is stretched out without wearying — my soul refuses to be comforted.

3 When I remember Creator, I moan — when I meditate, my spirit faints.

4 You hold my eyelids open — I am so troubled that I cannot speak.

5 I consider the days of old, the years long ago.

6 I said, Let me remember my song in the night — let me meditate in my heart. Then my spirit made a diligent search:

7 Will YHWH spurn forever, and never again be favorable?

8 Has his steadfast love forever ceased? Are his promises at an end for all time?

9 Has Creator forgotten to be gracious? Has he in anger shut up his compassion?

10 Then I said, I will appeal to this — to the years of the right hand of the Most High.

11 I will remember the deeds of YHWH — yes, I will remember your wonders of old.

12 I will ponder all your work, and meditate on your mighty deeds.

13 Your way, O Creator, is holy. What god is great like our Creator?

14 You are the Creator who works wonders — you have made known your might among the peoples.

15 You with your arm redeemed your people, the children of Yaakov and Yosef.

16 When the waters saw you, O Creator, when the waters saw you, they were afraid — indeed, the deep trembled.

17 The clouds poured out water — the skies gave forth thunder — your arrows flashed on every side.

18 The crash of your thunder was in the whirlwind — your lightnings lighted up the world — the earth trembled and shook.

19 Your way was through the sea, your path through the great waters — yet your footprints were unseen.

20 You led your people like a flock by the hand of Moshe and Aharon.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 77 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm moves from the darkest night of the soul — verses 1-9 — to the decisive turn of remembering YHWH's past deeds — verses 11-20. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 77 in full. The recounting of the Exodus in verses 16-20 — the waters trembling, the thunder and lightning, the unseen path through the sea — is read as the covenant community's anchor in times of spiritual darkness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 77. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — your way was through the sea, your path through the great waters — yet your footprints were unseen. YHWH's path through the sea left no trace. He acted powerfully and invisibly. The psalmist in verses 1-9 cannot find YHWH — his footprints are unseen in the night of trouble as they were unseen in the sea. Yet the Exodus happened. The people crossed. YHWH was there. The lesson of verse 19 for the psalmist's present darkness: YHWH acts where his footprints cannot be seen. His absence is not abandonment.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 78 A Maskil of Asaph — Give Ear, O My People — The Teaching Psalm

1 Give ear, O my people, to my teaching — incline your ears to the words of my mouth.

2 I will open my mouth in a parable — I will utter dark sayings from of old,

3 Things that we have heard and known, that our fathers have told us.

4 We will not hide them from their children, but tell to the coming generation the glorious deeds of YHWH, and his might, and the wonders that he has done.

5 He established a testimony in Yaakov and appointed a Torah in Yisra'EL, which he commanded our fathers to teach to their children,

6 That the next generation might know them, the children yet unborn, and arise and tell them to their children,

7 So that they should set their hope in Creator and not forget the works of Creator, but keep his commandments.

8 And that they should not be like their fathers, a stubborn and rebellious generation, a generation whose heart was not steadfast, whose spirit was not faithful to Creator.

9 The Efrayimites, armed with the bow, turned back on the day of battle.

10 They did not keep Creator's covenant, but refused to walk according to his Torah.

11 They forgot his works and the wonders that he had shown them.

12 In the sight of their fathers he performed wonders in the land of Egypt, in the fields of Tzoan.

13 He divided the sea and let them pass through it, and made the waters stand like a heap.

14 In the daytime he led them with a cloud, and all the night with a fiery light.

15 He split rocks in the wilderness and gave them drink abundantly as from the deep.

16 He made streams come out of the rock and caused waters to flow down like rivers.

17 Yet they sinned still more against him, rebelling against the Most High in the desert.

18 They tested Creator in their heart by demanding the food they craved.

19 They spoke against Creator, saying, Can Creator spread a table in the wilderness?

20 He struck the rock so that water gushed out and streams overflowed. Can he also give bread or provide meat for his people?

21 Therefore, when YHWH heard, he was full of wrath — a fire was kindled against Yaakov — his anger rose against Yisra'EL,

22 Because they did not believe in Creator and did not trust his saving power.

23 Yet he commanded the skies above and opened the doors of heaven,

24 And he rained down on them manna to eat and gave them the grain of heaven.

25 Man ate of the bread of the angels — he sent them food in abundance.

26 He caused the east wind to blow in the heavens, and by his power he led out the south wind.

27 He rained meat on them like dust, winged birds like the sand of the seas.

28 He let them fall in the midst of their camp, all around their dwellings.

29 And they ate and were well filled, for he gave them what they craved.

30 But before they had satisfied their craving, while the food was still in their mouths,

31 The anger of Creator rose against them, and he killed the strongest of them and laid low the young men of Yisra'EL.

32 In spite of all this, they still sinned — despite his wonders, they did not believe.

33 So he made their days vanish like a breath, and their years in terror.

34 When he killed them, they sought him — they repented and sought Creator earnestly.

35 They remembered that Creator was their rock, the Most High Creator their redeemer.

36 But they flattered him with their mouths — they lied to him with their tongues.

37 Their heart was not steadfast toward him — they were not faithful to his covenant.

38 Yet he, being compassionate, atoned for their iniquity and did not destroy them — he restrained his anger often and did not stir up all his wrath.

39 He remembered that they were but flesh, a wind that passes and comes not again.

40 How often they rebelled against him in the wilderness and grieved him in the desert.

41 They tested Creator again and again and provoked the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

42 They did not remember his power or the day when he redeemed them from the foe,

43 When he performed his signs in Egypt and his marvels in the fields of Tzoan.

44 He turned their rivers to blood, so that they could not drink of their streams.

45 He sent among them swarms of flies, which devoured them, and frogs, which destroyed them.

46 He gave their crops to the destroying locust and the fruit of their labor to the locust.

47 He destroyed their vines with hail and their sycamores with frost.

48 He gave over their cattle to the hail and their flocks to thunderbolts.

49 He let loose on them his burning anger, wrath, indignation, and distress, a company of destroying angels.

50 He made a path for his anger — he did not spare them from death, but gave their lives over to the plague.

51 He struck down every firstborn in Egypt, the firstfruits of their strength in the tents of Ham.

52 Then he led out his people like sheep and guided them in the wilderness like a flock.

53 He led them in safety, so that they were not afraid, but the sea overwhelmed their enemies.

54 And he brought them to his holy land, to the mountain which his right hand had won.

55 He drove out nations before them — he apportioned them for a possession and settled the tribes of Yisra'EL in their tents.

56 Yet they tested and rebelled against the Most High Creator and did not keep his testimonies,

57 But turned away and acted treacherously like their fathers — they twisted like a deceitful bow.

58 For they provoked him to anger with their high places — they moved him to jealousy with their idols.

59 When Creator heard, he was full of wrath, and he utterly rejected Yisra'EL.

60 He forsook his dwelling at Shiloh, the tent where he dwelt among mankind,

61 And delivered his power to captivity, his glory to the hand of the foe.

62 He gave his people over to the sword and vented his wrath on his heritage.

63 Fire devoured their young men, and their young women had no marriage song.

64 Their priests fell by the sword, and their widows made no lamentation.

65 Then YHWH awoke as from sleep, like a strong man shouting because of wine.

66 And he put his adversaries to rout — he put them to everlasting shame.

67 He rejected the tent of Yosef — he did not choose the tribe of Efrayim,

68 But he chose the tribe of Yehudah, Mount Tziyon, which he loves.

69 He built his sanctuary like the high heavens, like the earth, which he has founded forever.

70 He chose Dawid his servant and took him from the sheepfolds.

71 From following the nursing ewes he brought him to shepherd Yaakov his people, Yisra'EL his inheritance.

72 With upright heart he shepherded them and guided them with his skillful hand.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 78 is the second longest psalm in the Tehillim and one of the most extensive historical recitations in the entire Hebrew canon. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 78 in full. The sustained historical meditation — from Egypt through the wilderness to Dawid — is read as the covenant community's master narrative: YHWH's faithfulness persisting through generation after generation of rebellion.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 78. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 38 — yet he, being compassionate, atoned for their iniquity and did not destroy them — he restrained his anger often and did not stir up all his wrath. The entire sweep of Psalm 78 — seventy-two verses of rebellion, testing, idolatry, and punishment — returns again and again to this: YHWH's compassion restraining his wrath. He remembered they were but flesh — verse 39. The covenant theology of Shemot 34:6-7 — slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love — is demonstrated across an entire nation's history. Verse 72 — with upright heart he shepherded them and guided them with his skillful hand. The psalm that began with the command to tell children of YHWH's deeds closes with the shepherd-king Dawid as the fulfillment of YHWH's covenant faithfulness to his people.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 79 A Psalm of Asaph — O Creator, the Nations Have Come — How Long?

1 O Creator, the nations have come into your inheritance — they have defiled your holy temple — they have laid Yerushalaym in ruins.

2 They have given the bodies of your servants to the birds of the heavens for food, the flesh of your faithful to the beasts of the earth.

3 They have poured out their blood like water all around Yerushalaym, and there was no one to bury them.

4 We have become a taunt to our neighbors, mocked and derided by those around us.

5 How long, O YHWH? Will you be angry forever? Will your jealousy burn like fire?

6 Pour out your anger on the nations that do not know you, and on the kingdoms that do not call upon your name.

7 For they have devoured Yaakov and laid waste his habitation.

8 Do not remember against us our former iniquities — let your compassion come speedily to meet us, for we are brought very low.

9 Help us, O Creator of our salvation, for the glory of your name — deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name's sake.

10 Why should the nations say, Where is their Creator? Let the avenging of the outpoured blood of your servants be known among the nations before our eyes.

11 Let the groans of the prisoners come before you — according to your great power, preserve those doomed to die.

12 Return sevenfold into the lap of our neighbors the taunts with which they have taunted you, O YHWH.

13 But we your people, the sheep of your pasture, will give thanks to you forever — from generation to generation we will recount your praise.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 79 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a communal lament over the destruction of Yerushalaym and the temple — paralleling Psalm 74. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 79 in full. Verse 9 — help us, O Creator of our salvation, for the glory of your name — is read as the definitive form of the covenant appeal: YHWH acts for his own name's sake, not merely for the people's sake.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 79. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name's sake. The appeal is to YHWH's own reputation among the nations. The destruction of Yerushalaym has become a taunt — verse 4 — and verse 10 asks: why should the nations say, where is their Creator? YHWH's honor is at stake in the fate of his people. This is the same argument Moshe used after the golden calf — Shemot 32:12 — if you destroy them, the Egyptians will say you brought them out for evil. YHWH's name and his people's welfare are inseparable in covenant theology.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 80 Of Asaph — To the Choirmaster — A Testimony — Give Ear, O Shepherd of Yisra'EL — Restore Us

1 Give ear, O Shepherd of Yisra'EL, you who lead Yosef like a flock — you who are enthroned upon the cherubim, shine forth.

2 Before Efrayim and Binyamin and Menasheh, stir up your might and come to save us.

3 Restore us, O Creator — let your face shine, that we may be saved.

4 O YHWH Creator of hosts, how long will you be angry with your people's prayers?

5 You have fed them with the bread of tears and given them tears to drink in full measure.

6 You make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves.

7 Restore us, O Creator of hosts — let your face shine, that we may be saved.

8 You brought a vine out of Egypt — you drove out the nations and planted it.

9 You cleared the ground for it — it took deep root and filled the land.

10 The mountains were covered with its shade, the mighty cedars with its branches.

11 It sent out its branches to the sea and its shoots to the River.

12 Why then have you broken down its walls, so that all who pass along the way pluck its fruit?

13 The boar from the forest ravages it, and all that move in the field feed on it.

14 Turn again, O Creator of hosts — look down from heaven, and see — have regard for this vine,

15 The stock that your right hand planted, and for the son whom you made strong for yourself.

16 They have burned it with fire — they have cut it down — may they perish at the rebuke of your face.

17 But let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself.

18 Then we shall not turn back from you — give us life, and we will call upon your name.

19 Restore us, O YHWH Creator of hosts — let your face shine, that we may be saved.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 80 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The refrain — restore us, O Creator, let your face shine, that we may be saved — appears in verses 3, 7, and 19, structuring the psalm in three movements. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 80 in full. The vine allegory — YHWH bringing a vine out of Egypt, planting it, and watching it destroyed — is read as the covenant history of Yisra'EL compressed into an agricultural parable.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 80. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — give ear, O Shepherd of Yisra'EL, you who lead Yosef like a flock — you who are enthroned upon the cherubim. Two images of YHWH in one verse: the intimate shepherd leading the flock, and the cosmic king enthroned upon the cherubim — the same throne imagery of Chanokh's throne vision and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice. Verse 17 — let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself. The son of man of verse 17 connects to the son of man imagery of Daniyel 7 and the Book of Parables of Chanokh — the figure at YHWH's right hand who embodies the covenant people's hope.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 81 Of Asaph — To the Choirmaster — Sing Aloud to Creator — I Would Feed You With the Finest Wheat

1 Sing aloud to Creator our strength — shout for joy to the Creator of Yaakov.

2 Raise a song — sound the tambourine, the sweet lyre with the harp.

3 Blow the trumpet at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day.

4 For it is a statute for Yisra'EL, a rule of the Creator of Yaakov.

5 He made it a decree in Yosef when he went out over the land of Egypt. I hear a language I had not known.

6 I relieved your shoulder of the burden — your hands were freed from the basket.

7 In distress you called, and I delivered you — I answered you in the secret place of thunder — I tested you at the waters of Merivah.

8 Hear, O my people, while I admonish you — O Yisra'EL, if you would but listen to me.

9 There shall be no strange god among you — you shall not bow down to a foreign god.

10 I am YHWH your Creator, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it.

11 But my people did not listen to my voice — Yisra'EL would not submit to me.

12 So I gave them over to their stubborn hearts, to follow their own counsels.

13 Oh, that my people would listen to me, that Yisra'EL would walk in my ways.

14 I would soon subdue their enemies and turn my hand against their foes.

15 Those who hate YHWH would cringe toward him, and their fate would last forever.

16 But he would feed you with the finest of the wheat, and with honey from the rock I would satisfy you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 81 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a festival liturgy — connected to the feast of Sukkot or Pesach — in which YHWH speaks directly to his people in a covenant lawsuit format. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 81 in full. Verse 10 — I am YHWH your Creator, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it — is read as the covenant invitation: YHWH who delivered is the same YHWH who provides.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 81. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — so I gave them over to their stubborn hearts, to follow their own counsels. The most terrifying judgment in Psalm 81 is not fire or sword — it is abandonment to the human will. YHWH steps back and lets the stubborn heart have what it chose. The consequence of refusing to listen to YHWH is being left to oneself. Verse 13-16 — oh, that my people would listen to me. The longing of YHWH for his people's obedience is expressed with the same anguish as a parent watching a child choose destruction. If they would listen — he would subdue enemies, feed them with finest wheat, satisfy them with honey from the rock.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 82 A Psalm of Asaph — Creator Stands in the Divine Assembly — Judge the Poor and Fatherless

1 Creator has taken his place in the divine council — in the midst of the gods he holds judgment.

2 How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked?

3 Give justice to the weak and the fatherless — maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute.

4 Rescue the weak and the needy — deliver them from the hand of the wicked.

5 They have neither knowledge nor understanding — they walk about in darkness — all the foundations of the earth are shaken.

6 I said, You are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you.

7 Nevertheless, like men you shall die, and fall like any prince.

8 Arise, O Creator, judge the earth — for you shall inherit all the nations.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 82 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm depicts YHWH standing in the divine council — the same heavenly assembly described in the Books of Chanokh and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice — to judge those charged with governing justly. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 82 in full. The divine council imagery is read as consistent with the Beta Israel tradition's understanding of YHWH as sovereign over all spiritual beings and all earthly authorities.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 82. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — Creator has taken his place in the divine council — in the midst of the gods he holds judgment. The divine council — the same heavenly assembly of Chanokh 1, the War Scroll's angelic hosts, and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice — is here convened for a judgment of cosmic injustice. The gods — elohim — in verse 1 and verse 6 are the heavenly beings charged with governing the nations. They have failed. They have shown partiality to the wicked. Verse 7 — nevertheless, like men you shall die. The beings of the divine council who have perverted justice will be stripped of their immortality. Verse 8 — arise, O Creator, judge the earth — for you shall inherit all the nations. The ultimate claim: YHWH alone governs all.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 83 A Song — A Psalm of Asaph — O Creator, Do Not Keep Silence — Your Enemies Are in Uproar

1 O Creator, do not keep silence — do not be quiet, O Creator, and do not be still.

2 For behold, your enemies make an uproar — those who hate you have raised their heads.

3 They lay crafty plans against your people — they consult together against your treasured ones.

4 They say, Come, let us wipe them out as a nation — let the name of Yisra'EL be remembered no more.

5 For they conspire with one accord — against you they make a covenant —

6 The tents of Edom and the Yishma'elites, Moav and the Hagrites,

7 Geval and Ammon and Amalek, Peleshet with the inhabitants of Tzor.

8 Asshur also has joined them — they are the strong arm of the children of Lot.

9 Do to them as you did to Midyan, as to Sisera and Yavin at the river Kishon,

10 Who were destroyed at En-Dor, who became dung for the ground.

11 Make their nobles like Orev and Ze'ev, all their princes like Zevach and Tzalmunna,

12 Who said, Let us take possession for ourselves of the pastures of Creator.

13 O my Creator, make them like whirling dust, like chaff before the wind.

14 As fire consumes the forest, as the flame sets the mountains ablaze,

15 So may you pursue them with your tempest and terrify them with your hurricane.

16 Fill their faces with shame, that they may seek your name, O YHWH.

17 Let them be put to shame and dismayed forever — let them perish in disgrace,

18 That they may know that you alone, whose name is YHWH, are the Most High over all the earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 83 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a communal prayer against a coalition of nations conspiring to destroy Yisra'EL — the most extensive list of enemy nations in the Tehillim. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 83 in full. The coalition of enemies in verses 6-8 — ten nations arrayed against Yisra'EL — is read in connection with the War Scroll's battle schedule against the nations allied with Belial.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 83. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18 — that they may know that you alone, whose name is YHWH, are the Most High over all the earth. The purpose of the judgment is not merely the destruction of enemies but their knowledge of YHWH's exclusive sovereignty. Even the prayer for defeat of the nations is aimed at their recognition of YHWH. This is the covenant universalism of the Tehillim: YHWH's victories are meant to draw all nations to the knowledge of his name. The War Scroll's final victory similarly results in all nations serving YHWH — Psalm 47:9, Column XVII of 1QM.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 84 Of the Sons of Qorah — To the Choirmaster — A Psalm — How Lovely Is Your Dwelling Place

1 How lovely is your dwelling place, O YHWH of hosts.

2 My soul longs, yes, faints for the courts of YHWH — my heart and flesh sing for joy to the living Creator.

3 Even the sparrow finds a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, at your altars, O YHWH of hosts, my King and my Creator.

4 Blessed are those who dwell in your house, ever singing your praise.

5 Blessed are those whose strength is in you, in whose heart are the highways to Tziyon.

6 As they go through the Valley of Baka they make it a place of springs — the early rain also covers it with pools.

7 They go from strength to strength — each one appears before Creator in Tziyon.

8 O YHWH Creator of hosts, hear my prayer — give ear, O Creator of Yaakov.

9 Behold our shield, O Creator — look on the face of your anointed.

10 For a day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my Creator than dwell in the tents of wickedness.

11 For YHWH Creator is a sun and shield — YHWH bestows favor and honor. No good thing does he withhold from those who walk uprightly.

12 O YHWH of hosts, blessed is the one who trusts in you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 84 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a pilgrimage psalm — the longing of the worshiper for the presence of YHWH in the sanctuary. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 84 in full. The psalm is one of the most beloved in the Beta Israel tradition — the longing for the courts of YHWH and the declaration that one day in his presence is better than a thousand elsewhere.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 84. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — a day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my Creator than dwell in the tents of wickedness. The doorkeeper — the most humble role in the sanctuary — is preferred to the most comfortable life outside. The measure of good is not comfort but proximity to YHWH. Verse 6 — as they go through the Valley of Baka they make it a place of springs. The pilgrims transform the dry valley into springs by passing through it. Covenant presence with YHWH transforms even difficult terrain into places of refreshment.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 85 Of the Sons of Qorah — To the Choirmaster — A Psalm — Restore Us Again — Steadfast Love and Faithfulness Meet

1 YHWH, you were favorable to your land — you restored the fortunes of Yaakov.

2 You forgave the iniquity of your people — you covered all their sin.

3 You withdrew all your wrath — you turned from your hot anger.

4 Restore us again, O Creator of our salvation, and put away your indignation toward us.

5 Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations?

6 Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?

7 Show us your steadfast love, O YHWH, and grant us your salvation.

8 Let me hear what Creator YHWH will speak, for he will speak peace to his people, to his saints — but let them not turn back to folly.

9 Surely his salvation is near to those who fear him, that glory may dwell in our land.

10 Steadfast love and faithfulness meet — righteousness and peace kiss each other.

11 Faithfulness springs up from the ground, and righteousness looks down from the sky.

12 Yes, YHWH will give what is good, and our land will yield its increase.

13 Righteousness will go before him and make his footsteps a way.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 85 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm moves from past restoration — verses 1-3 — through present petition — verses 4-7 — to eschatological vision — verses 8-13. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 85 in full. Verse 10 — steadfast love and faithfulness meet, righteousness and peace kiss each other — is one of the most celebrated verses in the entire Tehillim in the Beta Israel tradition, read as the vision of the restored covenant order.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 85. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — steadfast love and faithfulness meet — righteousness and peace kiss each other. Four covenant attributes of YHWH — hesed, emet, tzedek, shalom — are personified and depicted as meeting and embracing. These four are the qualities YHWH declared of himself at Sinai in Shemot 34:6-7. In the restored covenant order they converge. The earth and heaven participate: faithfulness springs from the ground, righteousness looks down from the sky — verse 11. The vertical and horizontal dimensions of covenant reality are aligned.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 86 A Prayer of Dawid — Incline Your Ear, O YHWH — There Is None Like You

1 Incline your ear, O YHWH, and answer me, for I am poor and needy.

2 Preserve my life, for I am godly — save your servant, who trusts in you — you are my Creator.

3 Be gracious to me, O YHWH, for to you do I cry all the day.

4 Gladden the soul of your servant, for to you, O YHWH, do I lift up my soul.

5 For you, O YHWH, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love to all who call upon you.

6 Give ear, O YHWH, to my prayer — listen to my plea for grace.

7 In the day of my trouble I call upon you, for you answer me.

8 There is none like you among the gods, O YHWH, nor are there any works like yours.

9 All the nations you have made shall come and worship before you, O YHWH, and shall glorify your name.

10 For you are great and do wondrous things — you alone are Creator.

11 Teach me your way, O YHWH, that I may walk in your truth — unite my heart to fear your name.

12 I give thanks to you, O YHWH my Creator, with my whole heart, and I will glorify your name forever.

13 For great is your steadfast love toward me — you have delivered my soul from the depths of Sheol.

14 O Creator, insolent men have risen up against me — a band of ruthless men seeks my life, and they do not set you before them.

15 But you, O YHWH, are a Creator merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness.

16 Turn to me and be gracious to me — give your strength to your servant, and save the son of your maidservant.

17 Show me a sign of your favor, that those who hate me may see and be put to shame because you, YHWH, have helped me and comforted me.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 86 is the only psalm in Book III attributed to Dawid. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. Verse 15 quotes the covenant declaration of Shemot 34:6 — a Creator merciful and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 86 in full. Verse 11 — teach me your way, O YHWH, that I may walk in your truth — unite my heart to fear your name — is read as the definitive prayer for covenant integrity: the undivided heart fully oriented toward YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 86. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — unite my heart to fear your name. The divided heart of Psalm 12:2 — a heart and a heart — is the condition of the double-minded. The prayer of Psalm 86 is for the opposite: a heart made single in its orientation toward YHWH. Verse 15 — but you, O YHWH, are a Creator merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness. This is the direct quotation of YHWH's self-declaration at Sinai after the golden calf — Shemot 34:6. Dawid prays YHWH's own words back to him. The covenant person's deepest resource in prayer is YHWH's own revealed character.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 87 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Psalm — A Song — Tziyon the Mother of All Peoples

1 On the holy mount stands the city he founded.

2 YHWH loves the gates of Tziyon more than all the dwelling places of Yaakov.

3 Glorious things of you are spoken, O city of Creator.

4 Among those who know me I mention Rahav and Babylon — behold, Peleshet and Tzor, with Kush — this one was born there, they say.

5 And of Tziyon it shall be said, This one and that one were born in her — for the Most High himself will establish her.

6 YHWH records as he registers the peoples, This one was born there.

7 Singers and dancers alike say, All my springs are in you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 87 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm declares Tziyon as the mother city of all peoples — even the nations historically opposed to Yisra'EL are registered as born in her. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 87 in full. The declaration that Kush — Ethiopia — is among those born in Tziyon is of particular significance in the Beta Israel tradition, read as YHWH's registration of the Ethiopian people within the covenant community centered on Tziyon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 87. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4-5 — YHWH registers Rahav — Egypt — Babylon, Peleshet, Tzor, and Kush as born in Tziyon. The great empires and historic enemies of Yisra'EL are enrolled in YHWH's register as citizens of Tziyon. The covenant people's holy city becomes the mother city of all nations who know YHWH. Verse 6 — YHWH records as he registers the peoples, this one was born there. The same heavenly register — the book of the living — that appears in Psalm 69:28 and the Epistle of Chanokh is here the register of Tziyon's citizens from all nations. Verse 7 — all my springs are in you. The fountain of life is in Tziyon — in YHWH's presence.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 88 A Song — A Psalm of the Sons of Qorah — To the Choirmaster — A Maskil of Heman the Ezrahite — YHWH, God of My Salvation — I Call Out Day and Night

1 O YHWH, Creator of my salvation — I cry out day and night before you.

2 Let my prayer come before you — incline your ear to my cry.

3 For my soul is full of troubles, and my life draws near to Sheol.

4 I am counted among those who go down to the pit — I am a man who has no strength,

5 Like one set loose among the dead, like the slain that lie in the grave, like those whom you remember no more, for they are cut off from your hand.

6 You have put me in the depths of the pit, in the regions dark and deep.

7 Your wrath lies heavy upon me, and you overwhelm me with all your waves.

8 You have caused my companions to shun me — you have made me a horror to them. I am shut in so that I cannot escape.

9 My eye grows dim through sorrow. Every day I call upon you, O YHWH — I spread out my hands to you.

10 Do you work wonders for the dead? Do the departed rise up to praise you?

11 Is your steadfast love declared in the grave, or your faithfulness in Abaddon?

12 Are your wonders known in the darkness, or your righteousness in the land of forgetfulness?

13 But I, O YHWH, cry to you — in the morning my prayer comes before you.

14 O YHWH, why do you cast my soul away? Why do you hide your face from me?

15 Afflicted and close to death from my youth up, I suffer your terrors — I am helpless.

16 Your wrath has swept over me — your dreadful assaults destroy me.

17 They surround me like a flood all day long — they close in on me together.

18 You have caused my beloved and my friend to shun me — my companions have become darkness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 88 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. It is the darkest psalm in the entire Tehillim — the only lament that does not turn to praise or confidence. The psalm ends in darkness with no resolution. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 88 in full. The psalm is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the voice of the covenant person in the uttermost extremity — where even the covenant promises appear silent — as a valid and preserved expression of human suffering before Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 88. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 88 stands alone in the Tehillim as the psalm with no turn, no resolution, no final confidence. It ends with darkness — my companions have become darkness. The ancient editors of the Tehillim preserved it precisely as it is. The darkest human experience before YHWH — unanswered prayer, total isolation, apparent divine abandonment — is given canonical space. The Tehillim does not require suffering to resolve before it is voiced. Verse 1 — O YHWH, Creator of my salvation — the address is still to YHWH. Even in total darkness the psalmist speaks to YHWH. The orientation toward YHWH persists even when every other element of the covenant experience appears absent.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 89 A Maskil of Ethan the Ezrahite — I Will Sing of the Steadfast Love of YHWH — The Davidic Covenant and Its Apparent Failure

1 I will sing of the steadfast love of YHWH, forever — with my mouth I will make known your faithfulness to all generations.

2 For I said, Steadfast love will be built up forever — in the very heavens you will establish your faithfulness.

3 You have said, I have made a covenant with my chosen one — I have sworn to Dawid my servant:

4 I will establish your offspring forever, and build your throne for all generations.

5 Let the heavens praise your wonders, O YHWH, your faithfulness in the assembly of the holy ones.

6 For who in the skies can be compared to YHWH? Who among the heavenly beings is like YHWH?

7 A Creator greatly to be feared in the council of the holy ones, and awesome above all who are around him.

8 O YHWH Creator of hosts, who is mighty as you are, O YHWH, with your faithfulness all around you?

9 You rule the raging of the sea — when its waves rise, you still them.

10 You crushed Rahav like a carcass — you scattered your enemies with your mighty arm.

11 The heavens are yours — the earth also is yours — the world and all that is in it, you have founded them.

12 The north and the south, you have created them — Tavor and Hermon joyously praise your name.

13 You have a mighty arm — strong is your hand, high your right hand.

14 Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne — steadfast love and faithfulness go before you.

15 Blessed are the people who know the festal shout, who walk, O YHWH, in the light of your face,

16 Who exult in your name all the day and in your righteousness are exalted.

17 For you are the glory of their strength — by your favor our horn is exalted.

18 For our shield belongs to YHWH, our king to the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

19 Of old you spoke in a vision to your godly one, and said: I have granted help to one who is mighty — I have exalted one chosen from the people.

20 I have found Dawid, my servant — with my holy oil I have anointed him,

21 So that my hand shall be established with him — my arm also shall strengthen him.

22 The enemy shall not outwit him — the wicked shall not humble him.

23 I will crush his foes before him and strike down those who hate him.

24 My faithfulness and my steadfast love shall be with him, and in my name shall his horn be exalted.

25 I will set his hand on the sea and his right hand on the rivers.

26 He shall cry to me, You are my Father, my Creator, and the Rock of my salvation.

27 And I will make him the firstborn, the highest of the kings of the earth.

28 My steadfast love I will keep for him forever, and my covenant will stand firm for him.

29 I will establish his offspring forever and his throne as the days of the heavens.

30 If his children forsake my Torah and do not walk according to my rules,

31 If they violate my statutes and do not keep my commandments,

32 Then I will punish their transgression with the rod and their iniquity with stripes,

33 But I will not remove from him my steadfast love or be false to my faithfulness.

34 I will not violate my covenant or alter the word that went forth from my lips.

35 Once for all I have sworn by my holiness — I will not lie to Dawid.

36 His offspring shall endure forever, his throne as long as the sun before me.

37 Like the moon it shall be established forever, a faithful witness in the skies.

38 But now you have cast off and rejected — you are full of wrath against your anointed.

39 You have renounced the covenant with your servant — you have defiled his crown in the dust.

40 You have breached all his walls — you have laid his strongholds in ruins.

41 All who pass by plunder him — he has become the scorn of his neighbors.

42 You have exalted the right hand of his foes — you have made all his enemies rejoice.

43 You have also turned back the edge of his sword, and you have not made him stand in battle.

44 You have made his splendor to cease and cast his throne to the ground.

45 You have cut short the days of his youth — you have covered him with shame.

46 How long, O YHWH? Will you hide yourself forever? How long will your wrath burn like fire?

47 Remember how short my time is — for what vanity you have created all the children of man.

48 What man can live and never see death? Who can deliver his soul from the power of Sheol?

49 YHWH, where is your steadfast love of old, which by your faithfulness you swore to Dawid?

50 Remember, O YHWH, how your servants are mocked — how I bear in my heart the insults of all the many nations,

51 With which your enemies mock, O YHWH, with which they mock the footsteps of your anointed.

52 Blessed be YHWH forever — Amen and Amen.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 89 closes Book III of the Tehillim and is one of the most theologically complex psalms in the entire collection. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 89 in full including the closing doxology. The psalm is read as the covenant community's most profound wrestling with the apparent failure of the Davidic promise — the throne broken, the covenant appearing violated — held against the sworn faithfulness of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 89. Verse 52 — Blessed be YHWH forever — Amen and Amen — is the doxology closing Book III, matching the double Amen of Psalms 41 and 72 that closed Books I and II. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 89 is structured as a collision between YHWH's sworn covenant — verses 1-37 — and the apparent reality of its failure — verses 38-51. YHWH swore by his holiness — verse 35 — that Dawid's throne would endure like the sun and moon. Yet the throne is cast to the ground — verse 44. The psalmist holds YHWH to his own oath: where is your steadfast love of old, which by your faithfulness you swore to Dawid — verse 49? The psalm does not resolve the tension. It ends with the question still open, sealed only by the doxology of verse 52. The entire Book III ends not with resolution but with the unanswered covenant question — the same posture of waiting and trusting that Psalm 88 expressed in total darkness. The Tehillim does not pretend the covenant is easy.

END OF PART FOUR — PSALMS 73 THROUGH 89 — BOOK III COMPLETE

Volume Nine — Tehillim — Part Four
